title: The Internal Paper
setup:
There's an internal working paper circulating among senior associates — not official, not endorsed, written by two people you know are being positioned for the year-three cohort. It argues for a structural change to how AUREL's London practice runs its pipeline. The argument is good. It's also politically risky to be associated with it.

You've been asked to add your name as a co-author. It would go to three managing directors, including the one who challenged your Meridian assumption.

options.label: Add your name — own the position
options.text: You read it one more time, decide you agree with it, and tell them to add your name. You don't add caveats. You're either in or you're not.
options.innerMonologue: Your name is now attached to an argument that three MDs may not like. You've decided that being right matters more than being liked.

options.label: Contribute edits without taking authorship
options.text: You offer to sharpen two sections of the argument but decline the author credit. You tell them your contribution is the content, not the name.
options.innerMonologue: You made the paper better and removed yourself from its consequences. The authors will know. You'll know.

options.label: Decline and tell Dana it exists
options.text: You decline authorship and tell Dana about the paper — as information, not as a warning. You let her decide what to do with it.
options.innerMonologue: You've created a debt with the authors and a credit with Dana. Whether those are the right accounts to be managing depends on what happens next.

options.label: Ask Dana if she thinks you should sign it
options.text: You show her the paper and ask directly: would it be career-helpful or career-damaging to attach your name? She reads it for four minutes before answering.
options.innerMonologue: She told you what she actually thought, which is worth more than most things. You still had to make the call yourself.

designNotes: Positions the player on the political map relative to the MD cohort. Option A sets up a potential reward or consequence at Y3 annual review. Option C burns peer relationships. Option D is the strongest information play but delays the actual decision.
